title: Evaluating open LLMs for agentic analysis orchestration in a typical biomedical lab
abstract: Agentic tools — software environments where a large language model plans, calls external tools, executes code, and iterates with minimal human intervention — will run a substantial share of routine biomedical data analysis within the next few years. However, per-call inference cost on frontier models is the bottleneck and can add up quickly. Here, we tested whether a free, locally-runnable open-weight model could take over the repetitive execution steps at frontier accuracy. We used Claude’s Opus to author plans of increasing detail for per-sample variant calling, and ran six 2026-release open-weight implementer LLMs against those plans on a set of desktop GPUs. qwen3.6:27b reproduced frontier accuracy on every plan and matched Opus cell-for-cell on a 36-cell error-injection matrix. A sub-$2,000 Jetson or Apple Mac Mini sufficed for the implementer side. The open-weight model landscape evolves on the order of months, so the specific implementer recommended here will be superseded; we provide the plans, harness, scoring code, and per-cell artifacts at https://github.com/nekrut/LLM-eval-paper as a framework for re-evaluating future models.

paper_title: Evaluating open LLMs for agentic analysis orchestration in a typical biomedical lab

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to evaluate whether a free, locally-runnable open-weight model can execute repetitive biomedical data analysis tasks at frontier accuracy, specifically focusing on per-sample variant calling in a typical biomedical lab setting.
objective.evidence: Here, we tested whether a free, locally-runnable open-weight model could take over the repetitive execution steps at frontier accuracy.

knowledge_gap.answer: The paper addresses the gap in cost-effective execution of repetitive biomedical data analysis tasks using open-weight models, as current frontier models incur high per-call inference costs.
knowledge_gap.evidence: Frontier models from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google write quality working code for data analysis tasks, but they cost cents to dollars per call, resulting in rapidly ballooning bills.

answer: The use of a free, locally-runnable open-weight model to execute detailed plans authored by a frontier model.
The evaluation of open-weight models on lab-scale hardware for executing bioinformatics tasks.
The introduction of a recipe-implementer split for cost-effective data analysis.
evidence: We test whether a recipe authored once by a frontier model can be executed end-to-end by a free, locally-runnable open-weight implementer at frontier accuracy.
Our goal is to characterize the recipe-implementer split on a real bioinformatics task — per-sample mtDNA variant calling on four paired-end Illumina samples — across the lab-scale hardware tier.

inspirational_papers.answer:
- GeneGPT (2024) Their clustering pipeline inspired our filtering model. (Methodological precursors)
- BioCoder (2024) Provided a benchmark for bioinformatics code generation. (Experimental baselines)
- BioLLMBench (2023) Highlighted limitations in LLMs for bioinformatics tasks. (Papers with limitations addressed by this work)
inspirational_papers.evidence: Prior LLM-in-bioinformatics evaluations fall into three categories. The earliest measured factual recall on genomics question-and-answer benchmarks: GeneGPT [1]... BioCoder [3] graded 2,269 short bioinformatics functions against unit tests... BioLLMBench [4] scored GPT-4, Gemini, and LLaMA on 24 tasks and found LLaMA frequently failed to emit runnable code.

steps.input: Five computers spanning the lab-scale hardware tier.
steps.output: A setup for testing open-weight models on various hardware configurations.
steps.evidence: We assembled five computers spanning the lab-scale hardware tier (Table 3).

steps.step: Select and prepare dataset for variant calling.
steps.input: A small dataset derived from previous work on mutational patterns in human mitochondria.
steps.output: Four deeply downsized paired-end Illumina samples.
steps.evidence: We selected a small dataset [27] derived from our previous work on the analysis of mutational patterns in human mitochondria.

steps.output: Execution of variant-calling workflow on open-weight models.
steps.evidence: We developed a simplified version of a haploid variant-calling workflow... Each model is run under one of two conditions, which we call tracks.

steps.step: Evaluate model performance and error handling.
steps.output: Performance metrics and error-handling capabilities of models.
steps.evidence: To test how each model handles tool failures, we wrapped bwa and lofreq in short shell scripts (“shims”) that imitate the real binaries on disk.

tools.name: Claude-opus-4-7
tools.description: Used to author plans for variant calling.
tools.evidence: Every plan was authored by claude-opus-4-7 in the planner role of the recipe-implementer split.

tools.name: Qwen3.6:27b
tools.description: Used as the strongest implementer model for executing plans.
tools.evidence: The strongest implementer (qwen3.6:27b, 17 GB at 4-bit) is then cross-platform validated.

benchmark_datasets.name: Human mitochondria dataset
benchmark_datasets.data_description: Four deeply downsized paired-end Illumina samples derived from blood and cheek tissues.
benchmark_datasets.evidence: It contains four deeply downsized paired-end Illumina samples derived from blood and cheek tissues of a mother–child pair.

evaluation_metrics.name: Variant-overlap (Jaccard) score
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the per-sample Jaccard overlap of called variants.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to score every run on the primary metric.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: We score every run on a single primary metric — the variant-overlap (Jaccard) score.

evaluation_metrics.name: Error-handling metrics
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Classifies the script’s overall response to errors.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate error-handling capabilities of models.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: We score each error-matrix cell on three error-handling metrics — referred to here as the handle category, recover, and diagnose.

methods.name: Experimental design generation
methods.description: The approach includes producing experimental protocols, configurations, or evaluation strategies.
methods.evidence: We developed a simplified version of a haploid variant-calling workflow.

areas.evidence: Our goal is to characterize the recipe-implementer split on a real bioinformatics task — per-sample mtDNA variant calling.

performance_summary.evidence: Five of six 2026-release open-weight implementers reach mean score 1.000 on the v2 plan.

limitations.name: Single Workflow Coverage
limitations.description: The study covers only a single workflow, which may not generalize to other bioinformatics tasks.
limitations.evidence: First, our study covers a single workflow — per-sample mtDNA variant calling on a 16.6 kb reference.

limitations.name: Dependency on Closed API
limitations.description: Reproducibility of the recipes depends on Anthropic's continued availability of the model identifier.
limitations.evidence: Second, claude-opus-4-7 is an API-only model and reproducibility of the recipes themselves depends on Anthropic’s continued availability of that model identifier.

future_directions.name: Explore Larger References
future_directions.description: Investigate the recipe-implementer split on larger references and longer pipelines.
future_directions.evidence: The recipe-implementer split may behave differently on larger references, longer pipelines.

resource_link.evidence: we provide the plans, harness, scoring code, and per-cell artifacts at https://github.com/nekrut/LLM-eval-paper as a framework for re-evaluating future models.
